summary: The Maori are the indigenous Polynesian people of New Zealand (Aotearoa), whose ancestors migrated from Hawaiki aboard named waka (canoes) beginning around the 13th-14th century. Organized into iwi (tribes) and hapu (sub-tribes) that meet at marae, they are known for the haka war dance, ta moko tattooing, and a rich mythology centered on Rangi, Papa, and the trickster hero Maui. Maori history is dominated by 19th-century upheaval: the Musket Wars (sparked by chief Hongi Hika), the genocide of the Moriori people of the Chatham Islands, the 1840 Treaty of Waitangi with the British Crown, the Flagstaff War led by Hone Heke, the King Movement (Kingitanga) and the New Zealand Wars, and prophetic resistance movements like Pai Marire and Ringatu. Modern Maori history centers on land-rights activism, the Waitangi Tribunal, and a cultural and linguistic (Te Reo Maori) revival, alongside prominent literary depictions in novels like The Bone People and Once Were Warriors.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How the Maori are identified in giveaway lines: the indigenous Polynesian people of New Zealand, whose endonym for the country is Aotearoa ("land of the long white cloud"), the second-largest ethnic group in New Zealand after the Pakeha (European New Zealanders), traditionally concentrated in districts like Opotiki and Wairoa, who migrated to their homeland from Hawaiki aboard seven named great canoes (waka) such as Tainui, Mataatua, and Nukutere.

clues.clue: Indigenous Polynesian people who use "Aotearoa" ("land of the long white cloud") as the name for New Zealand; New Zealand's Black Caps cricket team played under this name in 2015, and it names their symbolic silver fern/koru
examples: name these Indigenous people who use the name Aotearoa for New Zealand

In 2015, the Black Caps played for the first time under a name in this language translated as "land of the long white cloud." give this language of the name Aotearoa that activists have fought to restore for New Zealand

clues.clue: Indigenous Polynesian ethnic group, the native/indigenous inhabitants of New Zealand
examples: name this Polynesian ethnic group, the indigenous people of New Zealand
name these indigenous Polynesian inhabitants of New Zealand

clues.clue: Their leaders signed the 1840 Treaty of Waitangi with the British Crown
examples: name this ethnicity whose leaders signed the Treaty of Waitangi, the indigenous people of New Zealand
name these indigenous peoples who signed the Treaty of Waitangi with the British

clues.clue: Second-largest ethnic group in New Zealand after the Pakeha, concentrated in districts including Opotiki and Wairoa
examples: They are the second largest ethnic group in their country after the Pakeha.
The districts of Opotiki and Wairoa have the greatest concentration of this ethnicity.

clues.clue: Legendarily migrated to their homeland from Hawaiki aboard waka (canoes); seven notable canoes are individually named in oral tradition, such as Tainui, Mataatua, and Nukutere, with the greatest reportedly taken by the "wife-stealer" Kupe
examples: These people legendarily migrated to their land on seven great canoes.

Name these objects, seven notable examples of which are called names like Tainui, Mataatua, and Nukutere in oral tradition. The greatest of these objects was supposedly taken by notorious wife-stealer Kupe.

clues.clue: A 99-year lease was granted on a geyser hotspot near the destroyed Pink and White Terraces to incentivize cultural tourism
examples: To incentivize cultural tourism, this ethnicity granted a 99-year lease on a geyser hotspot near the destroyed Pink and White Terraces.

clues.clue: The tree gum kapia, burned by these people as an insecticide, led to a lucrative kauri-gum mining craze in the mid-1800s
examples: A tree gum known as kapia that these people burned as an insecticide led to a lucrative mining craze in the mid-1800s.

clues.clue: Contact with Europeans declined after some of these people attacked the docked missionary ship Boyd; they were also the progenitors, and later enslavers, of the Moriori people of the Chatham Islands
examples: Missionaries halted efforts to convert these people after some of them attacked the docked ship Boyd.
Contact between this people and the Pakeha declined after the Boyd Massacre, and these people were the progenitors, and later enslavers, of the Moriori people of the Chatham Islands.

clues.clue: A member of this ethnicity filed a complaint about the book This Horrid Practice, which discussed cannibalism among them
examples: A member of this ethnicity filed a complaint about the book This Horrid Practice, which discussed cannibalism among them.

works.name: Musket Wars
works.description: A bloody arms race of inter-iwi conflicts fought largely in the 1810s-1830s, sparked when Ngapuhi chief Hongi Hika (who had toured England with missionary Thomas Kendall and personally received a suit of armor from King George IV) began trading for European muskets at Bay of Islands whaling stations, exchanging pigs, potatoes, and preserved tattooed heads (mokomokai). The introduction of firearms, aided by potato cultivation (taewa) that freed more men for warfare, necessitated new pa fortifications and caused devastating casualties; the wars are also linked to the 1835 Moriori genocide, carried out by tribes displaced during the fighting.

clues.clue: Named for the flintlock muskets, first obtained at Bay of Islands whaling stations in exchange for pigs, potatoes, and preserved tattooed heads (mokomokai), that sparked a bloody early-19th-century arms race among Maori iwi
examples: Hongi Hika led troops in what bloody, early 19th-century wars fought by Maori iwi with flintlocks?
The weapons used in these conflicts were obtained at whaling stations in the Bay of Islands in exchange for pigs and potatoes and necessitated new fortifications for pa villages.

clues.clue: Ngapuhi chief Hongi Hika, who toured England with missionary Thomas Kendall and was personally gifted a suit of armor by King George IV, is credited with starting the wars after obtaining the first muskets from the "Baron" de Thierry
examples: This man went with Kendall to England, where he personally received a suit of armor from King George IV.
These events are named for the objects that Baron de Thierry sold to a Christian convert ally of Samuel Marsden who was gifted a suit of armor by George IV while touring England.

clues.clue: Tribal infighting during the wars was accelerated by the cultivation of the potato variety taewa, which freed more men for war
examples: Warfare between members of this ethnicity may have been accelerated by the cultivation of potatoes called taewa, freeing men for war.

clues.clue: The wars caused two iwi, Ngati Mutunga and Ngati Tama, to be displaced and flee to the Chatham Islands, where they went on to massacre the Moriori people in 1835
examples: Two tribes of these people would also perpetrate the 1835 genocide of the Moriori population in the Chatham islands.
After losing their land during these conflicts, the Ngati Tama and Ngati Mutunga tribes sailed to the Chatham Islands, where they oversaw the Moriori Genocide.

clues.clue: Angela Ballara's history of the wars denies the "fatal impact" theory and is critiqued in Cannibal Talk by Gananath Obeyesekere
examples: Angela Ballara's work on these events denies the "fatal impact" theory, stresses continuity with events like the "fall of parrots," and is critiqued in Cannibal Talk by Gananath Obeyesekere.

clues.clue: In the "Elizabeth affair," James Stewart ferried warriors seeking utu (revenge) during the wars in exchange for a cargo of flax
examples: In the Elizabeth affair, James Stewart ferried men seeking utu during these events in exchange for a cargo of flax.

clues.clue: Kupe (a legendary ancestor-figure in Maori tradition) traded preserved tattooed heads during the Musket Wars
examples: Kupe was a legendary leader of these people, who traded preserved tattooed heads during the Musket Wars.

clues.clue: The Battle of Hingakaka, precipitated by a dispute over kahawai fish, saw the sacred relic Te Uenuku lost for about 100 years
examples: One engagement fought by this ethnic group, the Battle of Hingakaka, was precipitated by a dispute over kahawai and saw the sacred Te Uenuku become lost for about 100 years.

clues.clue: A missionary's journal gave a firsthand account of the wars while accompanying the Ngapuhi tribe on a war expedition
examples: A firsthand account of the Musket Wars was given in this missionary's journal written while he was accompanying the Ngapuhi tribe on a war expedition.

works.name: Treaty of Waitangi (1840)
works.description: Hastily drafted by Governor William Hobson and signed in 1840 by more than 500 Maori chiefs (rangatira), the treaty ceded "sovereignty" (kawanatanga) to the British Crown in exchange for the confirmation of Maori land and other rights, but the English and Maori-language texts (the Maori is often called simply Te Tiriti) diverged sharply on what "sovereignty" meant. An 1877 court case declared the treaty a "simple nullity," and a 1975 act established the Waitangi Tribunal to investigate breaches of it going forward.

clues.clue: Signed in 1840 between Maori chiefs and Governor William Hobson; hastily drafted, it had difficulty translating the English word "sovereignty," and its Maori-language text is often called simply Te Tiriti
examples: Leaders of this ethnicity signed an 1840 treaty hastily drafted by Governor William Hobson which had difficulty translating the English word "sovereignty."
the Treaty of Waitangi is written in English and what indigenous language of New Zealand?

clues.clue: Over 500 chiefs (rangatira) signed the treaty, ceding "sovereignty" while the Crown confirmed their territorial and property rights
examples: Captain Hobson got many chiefs of this ethnicity to sign a treaty that reaffirmed their territorial rights in exchange for acknowledging British overlordship.
Over 500 of them signed the Treaty of Waitangi.

clues.clue: An 1877 court case declared the treaty a "simple nullity"; the phrase in Maori for "absolute sovereignty" (which the treaty did NOT actually grant Britain) is now represented by a black, white, and red flag
examples: An 1877 court case, though, would declare that treaty a "simple nullity."
A controversial phrase in this language meaning "absolute sovereignty" is represented by a black, white and red flag.

clues.clue: A 1975 act established the Waitangi Tribunal to investigate breaches of the treaty
examples: Since 1975, a tribunal has investigated violations of an 1840 agreement by which these people allegedly ceded "sovereignty" to the Crown, the Treaty of Waitangi.
A 1975 act established a tribunal to investigate breaches of an 1860 treaty signed by chiefs of this ethnic group and William Hobson.

clues.clue: A chief cut down a British flag that he had previously gifted to James Busby; Busby had earlier negotiated the treaty (starting the Flagstaff War)
examples: A chief of these people declared war on settlers by cutting down a flag which he had formerly gifted to James Busby; Busby had earlier negotiated a 1840 treaty with these people.

clues.clue: An earlier 1839 agreement granted them tribal autonomy from the British Crown, prior to the 1840 treaty itself
examples: These people would receive tribal autonomy by treaty from the British Crown in 1839.

clues.clue: Following the treaty, the British Crown fought a series of wars against Maori tribes from 1845 to 1872 over rising settler numbers in the region
examples: The British crown would fight a series of wars against tribes of these people over rising settler numbers in the region from 1845 to 1872.

works.name: Moriori Genocide & the Chatham Islands
works.description: The Moriori, the pacifist original inhabitants of the Chatham Islands who observed the ancestor Nunuku's Law of nonviolence, were largely killed or enslaved in 1835 by around 900 members of the Ngati Mutunga and Ngati Tama iwi, who had been displaced from mainland New Zealand during the Musket Wars and hijacked a ship to reach the islands. An offshoot Moriori population's dendroglyphs (rakau, tree carvings) are protected by the Hapupu Reserve; a 1994 tribunal hearing formally recognized the Moriori as the Chatham Islands' original inhabitants.

clues.clue: The pacifist Moriori of the Chatham Islands, who followed the nonviolent Law of Nunuku, were massacred and enslaved in 1835 by around 900 Maori of the Ngati Mutunga and Ngati Tama iwi, who hijacked a ship after being displaced during the Musket Wars
examples: Most of these people were killed or enslaved in the 1830s by a group of 900 armed invaders who came over on a hijacked ship fleeing the Musket Wars.
These people narrowly survived campaigns by invaders who exploited these people's Law of Nunuku.

clues.clue: Dendroglyphs called rakau, made by an offshoot Maori population that moved to the Chatham Islands, are protected by the Hapupu Reserve; that population later massacred the Moriori
examples: Dendroglyphs known as rakau made by an offshoot population of this people that moved to the Chatham Islands are protected by the Hapupu Reserve, though that population was later massacred by the Taranaki.
These people massacred the pacific Moriori people of the Chatham islands.

clues.clue: Percy Smith and Elsdon Best promoted a racist theory that the Moriori were the last remnant of a paleolithic culture wiped out by a superior "Great Fleet" race; the 16th-century leader Nunuku-whenua is credited with the pacifist law
examples: Percy Smith and Elsdon Brest promoted the racist theory that these people were the last remnant of a paleolithic culture wiped out by a superior race's "Great Fleet."
Percy Smith's theory of the "great canoe fleet" attempted to cast the Maori as Aryan invaders who found this group already living on the islands.

clues.clue: Alexander Shand's conversations with Hirawanu Tapu are the major source for knowledge of the Moriori language, which used an "a" particle instead of the related Maori "e" particle
examples: Alexander Shand's conversations with Hirawanu Tapu are the major source for knowledge of these people's language, which used an "a" particle instead of a related language's "e" particle.

clues.clue: The Moriori are inaccurately depicted laboring on American-style plantations in the first chapter of Cloud Atlas; that chapter also depicts a Maori overseer whipping a Moriori slave named Autua
examples: These people are inaccurately depicted laboring on American-style plantations in the first chapter of Cloud Atlas.
A Maori overseer whips a Moriori slave named Autua in "The Pacific Journal of Adam Ewing," the first of six nested narratives comprising this novel.

clues.clue: In 1994, a tribunal hearing named for the town of Waitangi formally recognized the Moriori as the original inhabitants of the Chatham Islands
examples: In 1994, hearings of a tribunal named for this town notably recognized the Moriori as the original inhabitants of the Chatham Islands.

clues.clue: T. C. Winegard compared the Moriori genocide to Tasmania's Black War
examples: T. C. Winegard compared the Black War with this ethnic group's genocide of the Chatham Islands Moriori.

works.name: Flagstaff War / First Maori War (1845-46)
works.description: Also called the Northern War or Hone Heke's Rebellion, this conflict began when Ngapuhi chief Hone Heke repeatedly (four times) cut down the British flagstaff at Kororareka that he had earlier gifted to Resident James Busby, in protest of colonial encroachment; the Royal Navy ship HMS Hazard bombarded the town in response. Heke's side ultimately lost at the Battle of Ruapekapeka partly because many of his soldiers, converts to the Christianity brought by British missionaries, were absent observing Sunday church.

clues.clue: Ngapuhi chief Hone Heke cut down the British flagstaff at Kororareka (which he had earlier gifted) four times, starting the war; the ship HMS Hazard bombarded the town in response
examples: They fought the Flagstaff War under Hono Heke.
The ship Hazard bombarded a town controlled by members of this ethnicity after they cut down an object on a hill given as a gift to James Busby.
Name this 1845 war, often nicknamed for the object that chief Hone Heke cut down four times at the beginning of hostilities.

clues.clue: Also called the Northern War or the First Maori War; a related term is also applied to the "Great Northern War" between Charles XII of Sweden and Peter the Great
examples: Hone Heke cut down James Busby's flagstaff to start the First Maori War, which is often described with this term.

clues.clue: Heke's forces lost at the Battle of Ruapekapeka partly because many soldiers, who had converted to the Christianity brought by British missionaries, were absent attending Sunday church
examples: Hone Heke's loss at Ruapekapeka was partly due to the absence of many of his soldiers, who were attending Sunday church as practitioners of this religion brought to New Zealand by British missionaries.

works.name: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars
works.description: Founded in the 1850s to create a role among the Maori equivalent to a European monarch and to unify tribes against further land sales, the King Movement (Kingitanga) crowned its first king, Potatau I, with its cultural center at Waikato. Governor George Grey launched the "illegal" Waikato Invasion against the second king, and General Duncan Cameron crushed a related revolt in Taranaki; the movement's holders were later granted land back and, in 1995, a personal apology from Elizabeth II for the 1860s land confiscations that had targeted them. During World War I, Te Puea Herangi led the Kingitanga in opposing British conscription of Maori men.

clues.clue: Founded in the 1850s to create a role equivalent to a European monarch and unify tribes against land sales; its cultural and political center was at Waikato, with Potatau I as first king
examples: In the 1850s, a movement developed among these people to create a role in their society equivalent to European monarchs, the "King Movement."
Their most important cultural center is at Waikato, which was their capital under their first king, Potatau I.

clues.clue: Governor George Grey targeted the movement's holder in the "illegal" Waikato Invasion; General Duncan Cameron crushed a related revolt in the Taranaki region, with soldiers stampeding from a fort after being surprised by armed defenders emerging from bunkers
examples: Sir George Grey targeted this title's holder in the illegal Waikato Invasion.
These people attempted to raise a powerful monarch during their "King Movement." Duncan Cameron crushed a revolt of these people in the Taranaki region.

Possibly surprised by allies of a person with this title appearing from underground bunkers with double-barrelled shotguns, soldiers under Duncan Cameron stampeded out of a fort in a humiliating defeat.

clues.clue: Some kings refused the title by pointing to a mountain and saying it "does not move easily"; followers turned to peaceful resistance after warlord Rewi declared "we will fight on forever and ever and ever" at a losing battle
examples: Several people refused this title by pointing to a mountain and saying it "does not move easily." Followers of people with this title moved to peaceful resistance after a battle where the warlord Rewi declared "Friend, we will fight on forever and ever and ever."

clues.clue: In 1995, Elizabeth II personally apologized to the movement's holder (Tawhiao's successors) for 1860s land confiscations that had targeted it, in retaliation for actions like Tawhiao's annual marae tours
examples: The holder of this title received a personal apology from Elizabeth II in 1995 for 1860s land confiscations that targeted it in retaliation for actions like its holder Tawhiao's annual tour of marae and creation of the Kauhanganui parliament on the North Island.

clues.clue: Land confiscation (raupatu), a process undertaken as punishment for Kingitanga supporters who had sought to ban it (alcohol/alienation of land), targeted the movement
examples: Name this process undertaken as punishment for the supporters of the Kingitanga, which had sought to ban it. This process was known as "raupatu" by the people it affected.

clues.clue: During World War I, Te Puea Herangi led the Kingitanga in opposing British conscription, arguing Maori should not fight for a Crown responsible for the Treaty of Waitangi; a Pioneer Battalion of Maori soldiers nonetheless fought alongside forces commanded by William Birdwood at Gallipoli
examples: Te Puea opposed British conscription during World War I, believing that members of this ethnic group should not fight for a British crown responsible for the Treaty of Waitangi.
These people made up the majority of a "Pioneer Battalion" that fought alongside a force commanded by William Birdwood.
William Searancke's granddaughter was a leader of these people who was denounced as a "German sympathizer" for her efforts to oppose conscription.

clues.clue: The movement's founder of the "Hauhau" religion (Pai Marire) was a supporter of the King Movement, and Kingitanga holders warily granted sanctuary to the Ringatu religion's founder (Te Kooti)
examples: The founder of the "Hauhau" religion was a supporter of these people's King Movement.
Its holders warily granted sanctuary to the Ringatu religion's founder.

clues.clue: A parliament house, the Hikurangi, was founded by Maori at Papawai as part of a related self-governance movement
examples: A parliament house, the Hikurangi, was founded by this people at Papawai.

works.name: Prophetic Movements: Pai Marire, Ringatu & Ratana
works.description: A series of Maori-led religious/political movements arose in response to colonization: Pai Marire ("Hauhau"), founded by the prophet Te Ua Haumene, taught that shouting "Hapa" and erecting niu poles (after a vision of the angel Gabriel) would grant protection from bullets, a belief debunked when followers were massacred at Sentry Hill; a Pai Marire follower was blamed for hanging missionary Carl Volkner. Te Kooti founded the Ringatu church after leading a guerrilla campaign that began with a raid on Poverty Bay. The later Ratana Church calls for the rejection of spiritualists and tohunga (traditional experts) and has a strong presence among Maori.

clues.clue: Pai Marire ("Hauhau") founder Te Ua Haumene taught that shouting "Hapa" and erecting niu poles, after receiving a vision of the angel Gabriel, granted protection from bullets; the belief was debunked when followers were massacred at Sentry Hill
examples: A prophet among these people blamed for the hanging of the missionary Carl Volkner advocated for the erection of niu poles after receiving a vision of Gabriel.

Name this movement founded by Te Ua. Its members believed that Jehovah would protect them from bullets if they repeated the word "Hapa," a theory that was debunked when they were massacred at Sentry Hill.

clues.clue: A follower of the movement was blamed for the hanging of missionary Carl Volkner
examples: A prophet among these people blamed for the hanging of the missionary Carl Volkner advocated for the erection of niu poles after receiving a vision of Gabriel.

clues.clue: Te Kooti founded the Ringatu church after leading a guerrilla campaign against colonial forces that began with a raid on Poverty Bay
examples: One leader of these people founded the Ringatu church and was known as Te Kooti.
A guerilla campaign led by a leader of these people started with a raid on Poverty Bay.

clues.clue: The Ratana Church calls for the rejection of spiritualists and healers known as tohunga (bearers of expertise in culturally important skills, like ta moko tattooists) and has a strong presence among Maori
examples: The Ratana church has a strong presence among what people?
The Ratana Church calls for the rejection of spiritualists and healers known by this term.

works.name: Land Rights & Modern Political History
works.description: Twentieth- and twenty-first-century Maori political history centers on redressing land loss: activist groups like Nga Tamatoa ("the Young Warriors") and Te Hokioi ("Those with Foresight") emerged from Whina Cooper's 29-day 1975 Land March protesting land theft, and activists later occupied Raglan Golf Course and Bastion Point. The Waitangi Tribunal, established by a 1975 act, investigated grievances (including a fishing-rights case led by Eddie Durie against the Think Big synthetic-fuels plant), while government policy set a controversial billion-dollar "fiscal envelope" limit on settlement claims; special Maori parliamentary electorates (created in 1867) and the 1996 shift to MMP voting further shaped their political representation, and a Maori Party formed in 2004 to defend foreshore and seabed claims.

clues.clue: Nga Tamatoa ("the Young Warriors") and a group whose name means "Those with Foresight," led by Whina Cooper in a 29-day 1975 Land March, protested land theft by pakeha; activists later occupied Raglan Golf Course and Bastion Point
examples: A group representing people of this ethnicity with a name meaning "Those with Foresight" was led by Whina Cooper in a 1975 Land March.

In 1979, engineering students preparing for an offensive capping stunt were confronted by members of an activist group of this ethnicity whose name translates to "the Young Warriors." Whina Cooper led a 29-day march to protest the theft of land from these people by pakeha.
Activists from this ethnic group occupied Raglan Golf Course and Bastion Point after Whina Cooper led a 1975 march in support of this ethnic group's land rights.

clues.clue: The government set a controversial billion-dollar "fiscal envelope" limiting the total value of land claims
examples: The term "fiscal envelope" refers to a billion-dollar limit on the value of land claims by this ethnic group.

clues.clue: A 1975 act established the Waitangi Tribunal; under Eddie Durie, it sided with Maori fishing rights against a synthetic-fuels plant promoted by the "Think Big" economic strategy
examples: A tribunal led by Eddie Durie sided with this ethnic group's fishing rights in its rejection of a synthetic fuels plant promoted by the Think Big economic strategy.

clues.clue: A political party was formed in 2004 to defend Maori claims to New Zealand's foreshore and seabed, which are also protected by seats specifically allocated to them in the national legislature
examples: In 2004, a party representing this ethnic group was formed to defend its claims on its country's foreshore and seabed.
New Zealand's foreshore and seabed are subject to claims by this largest indigenous group, which has seats specifically allocated to it in the national legislature.

clues.clue: Four special Maori electorates, created in 1867, were indefinitely extended in 1876 and increased in 1996 following the report of the Royal Commission on the Electoral System, which established MMP (Mixed Member Proportional representation)
examples: The four special electorates temporarily awarded to people of this ethnic group in 1867 were indefinitely extended in 1876, and were increased in 1996 after the report of the Royal Commission on the Electoral System that established MMP.
After two referenda in 1992-93, New Zealand's legislature adopted this voting method in which overhang seats are awarded to make the seat count more proportional.

clues.clue: A national referendum rejected a proposed New Zealand flag design featuring their koru (unfurling silver fern) symbol
examples: A national referendum recently rejected a flag featuring this ethnic group's symbol of a koru, or an unfurling silver fern.

clues.clue: Under Prime Minister Helen Clark, affirmative-action policies benefiting Maori were opposed by the National Party; psychologist David Ausubel's Fulbright-funded research studied discrimination against them in the 1950s
examples: These people were the subject of affirmative action policies during the government of Helen Clark, which were opposed by the National Party.
A Fulbright grant funded psychologist David Ausubel's research on discrimination against these people in the 1950s.

clues.clue: Frederick Maning chronicled their history, and James Carroll became the first Maori to serve as acting head of government (Prime Minister) of New Zealand
examples: Frederick Maning chronicled the history of these people and James Carroll was the first of these people to serve as acting head of government of his country.

clues.clue: Gained universal suffrage in 1893; in conjunction with the Woman's Christian Temperance Union, they organized Nga Komiti Wahine to address issues like suffrage and prohibition
examples: In conjunction with the WCTU, these people organized Nga Komiti Wahine to tackle such issues as suffrage and prohibition. These indigenous peoples of New Zealand gained universal suffrage in 1893.

clues.clue: The Ngai Tahu division reclaimed a settlement of land on the South Island, which they call Te Waipounamu, in 1997
examples: The Ngai Tahu division of these people reclaimed a settlement of land on an island they refer to as Te Waipounamu in 1997.

clues.clue: Since some dialects merge "ng" with "k," Kai Tahu and Ngai Tahu are both terms for the same iwi
examples: Since some dialects of this language merge "ng" with "k," Kai Tahu and Ngai Tahu are both terms for one iwi.

works.name: Te Reo Maori (Language & Revitalization)
works.description: Te Reo Maori, an Austronesian language, uses a 15-letter alphabet including the digraphs "ng" and "wh" and features grammatical clusivity (distinct inclusive/exclusive "we" pronouns). After most speakers shifted to English in the twentieth century, revitalization efforts pioneered the immersion-based "language nest" (kohanga reo) model; the language became an official language of New Zealand as part of a broader cultural revival, though the proposed adoption of "Aotearoa" as the country's official name has drawn political opposition.

clues.clue: A 15-letter alphabet includes the digraphs "ng" and "wh"; the greeting kia ora comes from this language, whose name for New Zealand, Aotearoa, means "land of the long white cloud"
examples: This language's 15-letter alphabet includes the digraphs "N-G" and "W-H." The greeting kia ora comes from this language.
Name this language that is the source of an endonym meaning "land of the long white cloud," or Aotearoa.

clues.clue: After most speakers shifted to English in the 20th century, revitalization efforts pioneered the immersion-based "language nest" (kohanga reo) model, used in cities like Dunedin
examples: A phrase in this language referring to an immersion-based language revitalization strategy was translated into English as "language nest."
The revitalization movement of this ethnic group's language was the first to use immersion-based "language nests."

After most of this Polynesian language's speakers shifted to English during the twentieth century, recent attempts to revive it have involved placing children in immersive "language nests," or kohanga reo, in cities like Dunedin.

clues.clue: Features grammatical clusivity: distinct pronouns (matou vs. tatou) for "we" depending on whether the addressee is included
examples: Maori's pronouns matou and tatou exemplify this distinction, in which the word for "we" varies by whether or not the addressee is in the group "we" refers to.
Maori's personal pronouns feature three types of this grammatical feature.

clues.clue: Belongs to the Austronesian family and displays an ergative-type alignment, in which the subject of a transitive verb is treated distinctly from that of an intransitive verb
examples: Tagalog's two transitive voices are similar to the active voices of the ergative and the accusative. That unusual alignment is characteristic of the Austronesian family, which includes the language spoken by these native people of New Zealand.

clues.clue: Winston Peters opposed a proposal to adopt this language's word Aotearoa as New Zealand's official name
examples: Winston Peters opposes the proposal to adopt this language's word Aotearoa as the official name for a country.

clues.clue: A hill in the Hawke's Bay region has an 85-letter place name in this language, the world's longest single-word place name
examples: A hill in the Hawke's Bay region has an 85-letter name in this language.

clues.clue: Place names in this language include the Kilmog (from a phrase for a broom tea-tree species), the Clutha River region, the fjord Piopiotahi (named for the piopio bird), and the Hooker Glacier's neighboring peak, called "Cloud-piercer" (Mount Cook)
examples: The name of a hilly area called The Kilmog corrupted a phrase from this language for a species of broom tea-tree.
The Hooker Glacier borders a mountain named "Cloud-piercer" in this language, alternatively known as Mount Cook.

clues.clue: Became an official language of New Zealand as part of a widespread 20th-century cultural revival despite declining population
examples: Despite declining populations, this group would experience a widespread cultural revival through the 20th century, leading to their language becoming an official language of the country in which they live.

works.name: Ta Moko (Tattooing)
works.description: Ta moko is traditional Maori facial and body tattooing, cut into the skin with uhi (chisels) rather than punctured with a needle, leaving permanent grooves distinct from Western tattooing. Pigment (ngarahu) is made from bird or fish oil mixed with soot from burned kauri trees; men traditionally wear moko across the entire face, while the traditional style for women covers only the lips and chin. One origin story holds that the underworld spirit Niwareka's father taught the art to her husband Mataora after his pigment ran down his sweat-covered face.

clues.clue: Traditionally cut into the skin using uhi (chisels), leaving permanent grooves rather than being punctured with a rotary or coil gun like Western tattooing
examples: This art form is traditionally created using uhi, or chisels, which came from the underworld Rarohenga.
The indigenous practice of this medium differs from the more common western medium because, unlike using a rotary or coil gun, it leaves permanent grooves on the skin.

clues.clue: Uses a pigment called ngarahu, made from bird or fish oil mixed with soot from burned kauri tree wood
examples: This art form uses pigment called ngarahu made from bird or fish oil mixed with soot from the burned kauri tree.

clues.clue: One of the earliest styles is the kuri pattern, a lattice of three horizontal and vertical lines
examples: One of the earliest styles of this medium was the kuri, a pattern of 3 horizonal and vertical lines in a lattice.

clues.clue: One origin story holds that the underworld spirit Niwareka's father taught the art to her husband Mataora after sweat caused his pigment to run down his face
examples: One origin story for this medium is that the spirit Niwareka's father taught it to Mataora after sweat caused pigment to run down his face.

clues.clue: Men traditionally wear it across the entire face, while the traditional style for women covers only the lips and chin
examples: Men traditionally wear this body art on their entire face; the traditional style for women covers their lips and cheeks only.

clues.clue: Anthropologist Mead connected ta moko's facial patterns to Lapita motifs, alongside the Maori jade-carving tradition of pounamu
examples: Mead also connected Lapita motifs to patterns in ta moko face tattoos common among people of this ethnicity, whose jade carving tradition is called pounamu.

works.name: Culture & Customary Practices
works.description: Maori social organization is built on iwi (tribes) and hapu (sub-tribes), which meet at marae (meeting grounds) around wharenui (meetinghouses) decorated with the unfurling koru fern-frond motif; welcome ceremonies (powhiri) begin with a karanga summons and often include haka war dances, which are also performed before rugby games by the All Blacks and, traditionally, to herald the arrival of the goddess of summer. Genealogy (whakapapa), classified by Raymond Firth into four levels, lends personal authority (mana); pounamu (greenstone) is carved into ceremonial weapons like mere and was used to seal strong peace treaties. Musical traditions include microtonal moteatea chants (collected by politician Apirana Ngata), waiata songs, oriori lullabies, taonga puoro instruments like the conch-shell trumpet putatara, and poi (balls on strings twirled to music, whose "long" style was demonstrated on American television by geographer Evelyn Stokes).

clues.clue: Meeting grounds called marae host welcome ceremonies (powhiri) that begin with a karanga summons and are sometimes followed by haka war dances; associated meetinghouses (wharenui) are decorated with the unfurling koru fern-frond motif
examples: The karanga summons begin this ethnicity's welcome ceremony, or powhiri, which are sometimes followed by haka war dances.
These people's traditional meetinghouses are called wharenui, and they treasure pounamu greenstones.
...these people use an unfurling fern frond, known as a koru, to decorate their meeting houses.

clues.clue: Before rugby games, the All Blacks perform the haka, a rhythmic shout accompanied by feet-stamping; the dance was traditionally performed to herald the arrival of the goddess of summer
examples: Before rugby games, the All Blacks perform rhythmic shouts over feet stamping as part of this ethnicity's haka dance.
These people herald the arrival of the goddess of summer with a ceremonial dance called the haka.

clues.clue: Poi (two balls connected by string, twirled over musical accompaniment) is a performing art of this people; geographer Evelyn Stokes demonstrated its "long" style on American television
examples: In a performing art originated by this ethnicity, two balls are connected by a string and twirled over musical accompaniment.
The geographer Evelyn Stokes appeared on American television to demonstrate the "long" style of this ethnicity's art of poi swinging.

clues.clue: After three days of mourning at a marae, the recently deceased are buried in tangi funeral rites
examples: After three days of mourning at a marae, recently deceased members of this ethnicity are buried in tangi funeral rites.

clues.clue: Raymond Firth classified four levels of their genealogy (whakapapa), which is recited to enforce cultural identity and lend authority to personal mana
examples: Raymond Firth classified four levels of this people's genealogy, which is recited to enforce cultural identity and lend authority to personal mana.

clues.clue: Jade and similar minerals, called "greenstone" (pounamu), are carved into ceremonial weapons like mere; strong peace treaties were also referred to as "greenstone"
examples: Jade and similar minerals are widely used by these people to make ceremonial weapons like mere. These people use the term "greenstone" for jade and similar minerals.
These people referred to strong peace treaties as greenstone.

clues.clue: The microtonal chanting style moteatea was collected by politician Apirana Ngata; waiata songs are sung in Te Reo, and oriori lullabies/farewell laments may be choreographed with hand trembling and showing the whites of one's eyes
examples: Examples of a microtonal singing style of this ethnic group were collected by the politician Apirana Ngata; that chanting style is moteatea.
Waiata songs are sung by these people in the te reo language.
Name these people who adapted European tunes into "action songs." These people's oriori lullabies and farewell laments may be choreographed with hand trembling and showing the whites of one's eyes.

clues.clue: The taonga puoro instrument tradition includes a conch-shell trumpet called putatara, used in haka performances with body percussion at funerals and gatherings
examples: Name these Indigenous people who often sing and use body percussion for performances of haka dances, which are common at funerals or gatherings in New Zealand.
The Maori genre of taonga puoro often includes flute, percussion, and a trumpet called putatara made from these animal-derived objects.

clues.clue: Social units called iwi (tribes), which manage national parks like Tongariro, Fiordland, and Aoraki/Mount Cook, are made up of hapu and frequently operate marae; iwi took over more power from hapu during the 20th century
examples: Name these social units, which frequently operate meeting places called marae. During the 20th century, these social units took over more power from their constituent hapu.
Name this indigenous group whose "tribes," or iwi, help manage national parks such as Tongariro, Fiordland, and Aoraki/Mount Cook.

clues.clue: In pre-contact warfare, the first person killed (the "first fish") had his heart cut out and offered to the gods; in one campaign, 100 warriors were smuggled ashore wrapped in kuri-dog skins disguised as a beached whale
examples: During warfare between these people, the first person killed, called the first fish, had his heart cut out and offered to the gods.
During warfare, some of these people created a trojan horse-like beached whale by wrapping 100 warriors in the skins of kuri dogs.

clues.clue: Maori gardens (often containing Cordyline and made with ko sticks) traditionally centered on the kumara variety of sweet potato
examples: Maori gardens have historically centered on the kumara variety of this starchy staple vegetable that is now popular with bodybuilders.
Identify these places that often contain Cordyline. A book by Helen Leach traces the thousand-year history of these places, which are often made with ko sticks.

clues.clue: An earthwork pa fort was constructed on One Tree Hill, overlooking a city they call Tamaki Makaurau (Auckland)
examples: This ethnic group constructed an earthwork fort, or pa, on One Tree Hill that now overlooks a city they call Tamaki Makaurau.

clues.clue: Built terraced hill forts called pa, whose language was documented by missionary Thomas Kendall
examples: These people built terraced hill forts called Pa, one of which had its British flag torn down by Hone Heke.
These people built terraced hill forts called pa and had their language documented by Thomas Kendall.

clues.clue: In 1983, songwriters Ngoi Pewhairangi and Dalvanius Prime wrote "Poi E," the first Maori-language pop song, which outsold international artists in New Zealand and featured poi-swinging in its music video
examples: In 1983, Ngoi Pewhairangi and Dalvanius Prime wrote this first Maori pop song, outselling international artists in New Zealand.

works.name: Mythology
works.description: In the Maori creation myth, the primordial sky-father Rangi and earth-mother Papa were locked in a tight embrace (suffocating their children) until the forest god Tane forced them apart, later molding the first woman from red clay (or, in another telling, creating her from a pool of water reflecting the first man, Tiki). The trickster-hero Maui, son of Tangaroa (the sea god) and guardian of fish, fished up islands (including the North Island) with a magic hook baited with the blood of Mahuika's fingernails, from whom he had earlier tricked the gift of fire, before dying trying to win immortality via the vagina dentata of the underworld goddess Hine-nui-te-po. Tribal guardian spirits called taniwha protect groups, deities are depicted on carved "god sticks," and myths were first recorded in writing by governor George Grey; another cycle follows Tawhaki, who was nursed back to health with fire from a single giant log after his in-laws tried to murder him, then climbed a vine to heaven with his grandmother's aid and restored the sight of that cannibalistic thunder goddess.

clues.clue: The sky-father Rangi and earth-mother Papa were locked in such a tight embrace that their children suffocated, until the forest god Tane forced them apart to let in light
examples: In this culture's creation myth, the children of the Earth and the Sky conspire to push their parents apart with their feet to allow light into the world.
Her father and husband Tane pushed apart his parents the sky goddess Rangi and the earth mother Papa in this culture.
These peoples' sky-father and earth-mother lived in such a tight embrace that all their sons were suffocated until the forest god Tane forced them apart.

clues.clue: The trickster hero Maui, son of the sea god Tangaroa and guardian of fish, used a magic fishhook (baited with Mahuika's blood/fingernails) to pull up islands, including the North Island, from the sea
examples: This major figure in various Polynesian mythologies is the son of Tangaroa and the guardian of fish in Maori mythology.
Name this Polynesian hero who legendarily used his fishhook to pull up many islands out of the sea and is killed by the vagina dentata of Hine-nui-te-po.
They're not Hawaiians, but the mountains in part of this people's homeland were created when men cut into a giant fish that was caught with a jaw bone by the culture hero Maui.

clues.clue: Maui tricked the fire goddess Mahuika into giving him fire by taking her fingernails one by one; he died attempting to win immortality by passing through the underworld goddess Hine-nui-te-po, who is also called the ruler of the underworld and Tane's daughter/wife
examples: In a story from these people, humanity obtains fire after a hero tricks Mahuika into giving him her fingernails one by one.
In the myths of these Polynesian people, Hine-nui-te-po is the ruler of the underworld.

clues.clue: The first woman was created from a pool of water that cast a reflection of the first man, Tiki; deities are often depicted on "god sticks," and myths were first written down by George Grey while governor of New Zealand
examples: In this people's mythology, the first woman was created from a pool of water that cast a reflection of the first man, Tiki.
Deities worshipped by these people are often depicted on "god sticks." Several myths about these people were first written down by George Grey while he served as the governor of their home country.

clues.clue: Tribal groups are protected by fish- or reptile-like guardian spirits called taniwha
examples: In the mythology of this people, tribal groups are protected by fish- or reptile-like spirits called taniwha.

clues.clue: Tawhaki, after his in-laws tried to murder him, was nursed back to health with fire fueled by a single giant log, then climbed to heaven on a giant vine with the aid of his grandmother, a cannibalistic thunder goddess whose sight he restored
examples: One figure in this people's mythology restores the sight of his cannibalistic thunder goddess grandmother, who shows him how to climb the vines to heaven.
After an attempted murder by his in-laws, a figure sacred to these people was miraculously nursed back to health with the aid of a fire fueled by a single giant log.

clues.clue: The explorer-ancestor Kupe discovered the land under a long white cloud (Aotearoa) after eloping in an ocean-faring canoe and killing the monster Wheke
examples: According to this people's mythology, the explorer Kupe discovered a certain region located under a long, white cloud.
The myth of these people tells that Kupe eloped by fleeing in an ocean-faring canoe to found "Aotearoa," the land of the long white cloud, killing the monster Wheke in modern New Zealand.

clues.clue: The child-snatching monster Te Hokioi was likely inspired by encounters with the extinct Haast's Eagle; Kupe was the first ancestor to discover their homeland using waka
examples: The child-snatching Te Hokioi in this people's mythology was likely inspired by encounters with the Haast's Eagle. Kupe was the first of these people's ancestors to discover their now-homeland using wakas.

clues.clue: Tinirau, son of Tangaroa and guardian of fish, lent his pet whale to Kae for a return journey, only for Kae to kill and eat it
examples: This figure lends his pet whale to Kae for his return journey, only for Kae to kill and eat it.

clues.clue: The story of Tutira and Kawariki is found in their myths, which also feature the sea god Tangaroa and the primordial pair Rangi and Papa
examples: The story of Tutira and Kawariki is found in the myths of these indigenous people of New Zealand, who worshipped the sea god Tangaroa and the primordial pair Rangi and Papa.

clues.clue: An agricultural god used a secret stash of string to win the world's first kite-flying contest
examples: In a myth of these people, an agricultural god used a secret stash of string in order to win the world's first kite-flying contest.

clues.clue: The cave Taiwhetuki is the entrance to the underworld
examples: The cave Taiwhetuki is the entrance to the underworld in the mythology of this people.

clues.clue: The Milky Way is represented by the great fish Te Ikaroa, carried to the heavens by Kewa, a son of Papa and Rangi; this tradition also tells of Maui fishing up an island with a jawbone hook
examples: In this mythology, the Milky Way is represented by the great fish Te Ikaroa, which was carried to the heavens by Kewa, a son of Papa and Rangi.

works.name: Cultural Depictions in Literature & Film
works.description: Maori characters and culture feature prominently in New Zealand literature and film: Alan Duff's 1994 novel Once Were Warriors (adapted into a film by Lee Tamahori) follows Jake Heke's alcoholism and domestic violence against his wife Beth; Keri Hulme's Booker Prize-winning 1984 novel The Bone People, rejected by major publishers before being picked up by the feminist collective Spiral, follows the mute boy Simon (adopted by Joe and Hana Gillayley) and the artist Kerewin Holmes; and Witi Ihimaera's novel The Whale Rider is based on the story of Paikea, guided to the South Island by a whale. Katherine Mansfield's short stories, including "How Pearl Button Was Killed" and "The Garden Party," also feature Maori characters, and 2008 Neustadt laureate Patricia Grace was the first prominent female Maori writer.

clues.clue: Alan Duff's 1994 novel Once Were Warriors, adapted into a film directed by Lee Tamahori, follows Jake Heke's alcoholism and domestic violence against his wife Beth, whose eldest son joins the Brown Fists gang and who later starts a culture group after Grace hangs herself
examples: A character of this ethnicity loses contact with his eldest son, who joins the Brown Fists gang, and becomes homeless after discovering Grace hanged herself.
Beth starts a culture group of this ethnicity at the end of a novel in which Jake Heke drunkenly leaves his family, titled Once Were Warriors.
A novel about Jake's domestic violence against his wife Beth explores the effects of alcoholism in the ghetto population of this people; that novel was made into a 1994 film directed by Lee Tamahori.

clues.clue: Keri Hulme's 1984 novel The Bone People, rejected by every major publisher until picked up by the feminist collective Spiral, won the Booker Prize; it follows the mute boy Simon (adopted by Joe and Hana Gillayley), who befriends the artist Kerewin Holmes, who lives in a tower and gave up an unnamed profession
examples: A character who is partly this ethnicity lives in "The Tower" and discovers a sandal with a hole in its heel that belongs to a mute boy.
This is the ethnicity of the protagonist of a novel in which Simon is beaten by his surrogate father Joe Gillayley but develops a relationship with Kerewin, who rebuilds a marae.
Name this 1984 novel that was rejected by every major publisher until it was picked up by Spiral, a feminist collective.

clues.clue: Witi Ihimaera's novel is based on the Maori story of Paikea, who was guided by a whale to the South Island; it centers on a succession crisis in a Maori family and inspired the film Whale Rider
examples: The Whale Rider by Witi Ihimaera centers on the succession crisis of a family of these Indigenous people of New Zealand.
A novel by Witi Ihimaera is based on the Maori story of Paikea, who was guided by one of these animals to the South Island.

clues.clue: Katherine Mansfield's short stories, including "How Pearl Button Was Killed" and "The Garden Party" (in which Laura Sheridan's family hosts the title event), feature Maori characters
examples: People of this Indigenous group appear in stories like "How Pearl Button Was Killed" by Katherine Mansfield.
In this short story by New Zealand author Katherine Mansfield, Laura Sheridan's family hosts the title event and learns of the death of Mr. Scott, their neighbor.

clues.clue: 2008 Neustadt Prize winner Patricia Grace was the first prominent female Maori writer; Witi Ihimaera and Grace have both published fiction in the University of Hawaii's Talanoa literature series
examples: 2008 Neustadt winner Patricia Grace is the first prominent female writer of this ethnicity.
The University of Hawaii's Talanoa literature series has also published fiction by Witi Ihimaera and Patricia Grace, two members of this Indigenous ethnic group of New Zealand.

clues.clue: Ngaio Marsh, one of the four "Queens of Crime" alongside Agatha Christie, Dorothy L. Sayers, and Margery Allingham, explored Maori culture in her novel Colour Scheme, in which Maurice Questing's death in a mud pool is connected to a Maori legend
examples: In Marsh's novel Colour Scheme, Maurice Questing dies in a mud pool, which characters of this ethnicity relate to a legend of theirs.

clues.clue: Opera soprano Kiri Te Kanawa, who is partially descended from the Maori and starred as Maria in a 1984 "operatic version" of West Side Story, gave a controversial 2003 interview about them
examples: Name these people who were controversially criticized in a 2003 interview by an opera singer who is partially descended from these people. That musician descended from these people starred as Maria in the 1984 "operatic version" of West Side Story.

comprehensive_summary: The Maori are the indigenous Polynesian people of Aotearoa (New Zealand), whose ancestors legendarily migrated from Hawaiki aboard seven named great canoes (waka) such as Tainui, Mataatua, and Nukutere, with radiocarbon dating suggesting settlement occurred between the eleventh and thirteenth centuries. Organized into iwi (tribes) built from constituent hapu, Maori society centers on marae meeting grounds and wharenui meetinghouses decorated with the koru fern motif, welcome ceremonies (powhiri) beginning with a karanga and often followed by haka war dances (also performed today by the All Blacks before rugby matches), tangi funeral rites, and genealogical recitation (whakapapa) that lends personal mana; musical traditions include microtonal moteatea chants collected by politician Apirana Ngata, waiata songs, and the poi performing art. Ta moko facial and body tattooing, cut into the skin with uhi chisels using soot-based ngarahu pigment, is traditionally attributed to an origin myth involving the underworld spirit Niwareka and her husband Mataora. Maori mythology holds that the sky-father Rangi and earth-mother Papa were forced apart by their son, the forest god Tane, who later fashioned the first woman; the trickster hero Maui, son of the sea god Tangaroa, fished up islands including the North Island with a magic hook, tricked fire from the goddess Mahuika, and died attempting to conquer death via the underworld goddess Hine-nui-te-po, while the explorer-ancestor Kupe discovered the "land of the long white cloud" and the hero Tawhaki climbed a vine to heaven to restore his cannibalistic thunder-goddess grandmother's sight. The early 19th century saw the Musket Wars, a bloody arms race sparked by Ngapuhi chief Hongi Hika's acquisition of European firearms (traded at Bay of Islands whaling stations for pigs, potatoes, and preserved tattooed heads), which displaced the Ngati Mutunga and Ngati Tama iwi to the Chatham Islands, where in 1835 they killed or enslaved most of the pacifist Moriori people, who had followed the nonviolent Law of Nunuku. In 1840, over 500 Maori chiefs signed the Treaty of Waitangi with Governor William Hobson, ceding "sovereignty" to the British Crown in a document whose English and Maori texts diverged on that term's meaning; an 1877 court case later declared the treaty a "simple nullity." Ngapuhi chief Hone Heke's repeated felling of the British flagstaff at Kororareka, which he had earlier gifted to Resident James Busby, triggered the Flagstaff War (Northern War), in which Heke's forces were ultimately defeated at Ruapekapeka partly because many soldiers, having converted to Christianity, were observing Sunday church. In the 1850s, the King Movement (Kingitanga) crowned Potatau I to unify tribes against land sales from a base at Waikato; Governor George Grey's "illegal" Waikato Invasion and General Duncan Cameron's campaign in Taranaki targeted the movement, whose later holder Tawhiao received a personal 1995 apology from Elizabeth II for the resulting land confiscations (raupatu), and whose WWI-era leader Te Puea Herangi opposed British conscription of Maori men (who nonetheless served in a Pioneer Battalion under William Birdwood). Prophetic resistance movements followed: Te Ua Haumene's Pai Marire ("Hauhau") taught that shouting "Hapa" and erecting niu poles granted invulnerability to bullets, disproved at the Sentry Hill massacre, while Te Kooti founded the Ringatu church after a guerrilla campaign beginning with a raid on Poverty Bay, and the later Ratana Church gained wide Maori following. Twentieth-century activism included Whina Cooper's 1975 Land March and groups like Nga Tamatoa, occupations of Raglan Golf Course and Bastion Point, the 1975-established Waitangi Tribunal (which under Eddie Durie backed Maori fishing rights against the "Think Big" synthetic-fuels plant), a controversial "fiscal envelope" limit on settlement claims, dedicated parliamentary electorates expanded after the 1996 shift to MMP voting, and a 2004 political party formed over foreshore-and-seabed claims; Te Reo Maori itself underwent revitalization through the pioneering "language nest" (kohanga reo) immersion model after most speakers had shifted to English. Maori culture and history are the subject of prominent New Zealand literature, including Alan Duff's Once Were Warriors (filmed by Lee Tamahori), Keri Hulme's Booker Prize-winning The Bone People, Witi Ihimaera's The Whale Rider, and short stories by Katherine Mansfield, alongside notable figures such as Neustadt Prize-winning author Patricia Grace, detective novelist Ngaio Marsh, and opera soprano Kiri Te Kanawa.
recursive_suggestions: Treaty of Waitangi — appeared as its own answerline in a bonus part; rich standalone detail on the drafting, translation disputes, and later tribunal history could support a dedicated page
Musket Wars — appeared as its own answerline; detailed inter-iwi conflict history (Hongi Hika, the Elizabeth affair, Battle of Hingakaka) could support its own page

Moriori — appeared as its own answerline; the Chatham Islands genocide has enough distinct detail (Nunuku's Law, Percy Smith's racist theory, Cloud Atlas depiction) for a standalone page — filter for History or a dedicated ethnic-group category

Maori language (Te Reo Maori) — appeared as its own answerline; linguistic detail (clusivity, ergativity, alphabet, revitalization) could support its own page, though much content overlaps with this page's Language section
Maori King / Kingitanga — appeared as its own answerline with dense detail (Tawhiao, Waikato Invasion, 1995 apology) that could support a standalone page
Ta moko — a single question with substantial specific detail (uhi chisels, ngarahu pigment, Niwareka/Mataora origin myth) that could support its own page if more clues surface
The Bone People (Keri Hulme) — recurring literary answerline with dense plot detail; check for its own dedicated Literature page
Once Were Warriors (Alan Duff) — recurring literary/film answerline; check for its own dedicated Literature or Film page
Maui — recurring Polynesian mythological figure with dense detail (fishhook, Mahuika, Hine-nui-te-po); check for a broader Polynesian/Maori mythology page distinct from this History page
Kiri Te Kanawa and Ngaio Marsh — both cited for their connections to Maori subject matter but are themselves notable New Zealand cultural figures who could merit separate biography pages
